Name: spectral_task_routing
Title: Spectral Task Routing: PAC-Bayes-Guided Compute Allocation for Latency-Aware Multi-Task Learning
Short Hypothesis: The spectral stability of task-specific representations in multi-task learning (MTL) serves as a direct proxy for computational difficulty, enabling training-free, latency-aware compute allocation that enforces strict wall-clock budgets while maintaining task performance.
Related Work: Existing work on training-free frameworks focuses on in-context learning (e.g., Geometric Interference Cancellation, Spectral Quantization Routing) and PCA-based filtering for robustness. While these methods leverage geometric properties, they are limited to single-task or ICL settings. This proposal extends these ideas to MTL, where tasks share representations but have varying computational demands. By linking spectral stability to PAC-Bayes generalization bounds, we provide a mathematically transparent framework for task-specific compute allocation, distinguishing from heuristic or static approaches.
Abstract: Multi-task learning (MTL) enables shared representation learning across tasks but faces challenges in resource-efficient inference, particularly under strict latency budgets. We propose Spectral Task Routing (STR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates compute resources based on the spectral stability of task-specific representations. We hypothesize that tasks with well-conditioned, rapidly decaying spectral subspaces require fewer computational resources, while ill-conditioned tasks necessitate higher precision or deeper processing. STR computes these geometric diagnostics on-the-fly using randomized SVD and maps them to a task-specific compute allocation policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral stability to generalization under computational perturbations. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across diverse MTL benchmarks, STR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic baselines. By replacing qualitative task difficulty assessments with formalized spectral risk bounds, STR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient MTL inference.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Validate the hypothesis that spectral stability correlates with task difficulty. Measure the spectral decay rate and condition number of task-specific representations across MTL benchmarks and correlate them with task performance under varying compute budgets.

Experiment 2: Implement Spectral Task Routing (STR) as a training-free framework. Use randomized SVD to compute spectral diagnostics on-the-fly and map them to a compute allocation policy. Evaluate on MTL benchmarks with strict latency budgets, comparing throughput, accuracy, and memory usage against static and heuristic baselines.

Experiment 3: Theoretical validation. Derive PAC-Bayes bounds linking spectral stability to generalization under computational perturbations. Validate these bounds empirically by measuring task performance under controlled perturbations.
Evaluation Metrics: Throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier, spectral decay rate, condition number, task-specific performance, memory usage.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Computational overhead of on-the-fly spectral diagnostics may offset latency gains. Mitigation: Use randomized SVD with low-rank approximations to minimize overhead.
Assumption that spectral stability fully captures task difficulty may not hold for all tasks. Mitigation: Validate across diverse MTL benchmarks.
Generalization to larger-scale MTL settings may require further optimization. Mitigation: Focus on single-GPU setups initially, with scalability as future work.
